title: 3D Volumetric U-Net
concept: 3D U-Net uses 3D convolutions to segment volumetric medical scans like MRI and CT volumes, preserving spatial context across slices.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main characteristic of medical image scans?

options.text: They are typically low-contrast, grayscale, and require high precision
They are high-contrast, colorful, and low-stakes
They are only processed by object detection boxes

exercises.explain: Correct! Medical scans are tricky grayscale images where precision is critical.

cards.title: The third dimension
cards.body: A CT scan or MRI isn't just a single flat photo. It is a stack of dozens of cross-section slices, forming a 3D volume of your body. If we segment each slice individually with a 2D model, we ignore the context above and below that slice. How do we build a model that understands the body in three dimensions?

cards.title: 3D U-Net
cards.body: The 3D U-Net is an extension of U-Net designed for volumetric data. It replaces all 2D convolutional, max pooling, and transposed convolutional layers with their 3D equivalents, allowing the network to process spatial context across all three axes.

cards.title: Slicing a loaf of bread vs. looking at the loaf
cards.body: 2D segmentation is like inspecting a loaf of bread by looking at individual slices one-by-one. 3D segmentation is like looking at the entire whole loaf, seeing how a raisin inside spans across multiple adjacent slices.

cards.title: MRI brain tumor tracking
cards.body: A brain tumor exists as a 3D mass. A 3D U-Net takes the full MRI volume (e.g. 128x128x128 voxels) and outputs a matching 3D mask, tracing the tumor's volume accurately across all slices.

cards.title: More dimensions aren't free
cards.body: You might think we should always use 3D models. However, 3D convolutions require massive amounts of GPU memory and compute. A single 3D volume can easily crash a GPU if the batch size is too large, forcing developers to crop volumes into patches.

left.text:
Processes slices individually
Ignores context between slices
Low memory footprint

right.text:
Processes entire volumes (voxels)
Preserves 3D spatial continuity
High memory & compute cost

exercises.prompt: What is the primary structural change in a 3D U-Net compared to a 2D U-Net?

options.text: It replaces 2D convolutions and pooling layers with 3D equivalents
It removes the decoder path to run faster
It uses dense classifier layers instead of convolutions
It only accepts color JPEG images

exercises.explain: Correct! 3D U-Net uses 3D convolutions to process volumetric data.

exercises.prompt: True or False: 3D U-Net requires less GPU memory than 2D U-Net because it processes slices in parallel.
exercises.explain: False. 3D convolutions are computationally heavy and require significantly more memory.

exercises.sentence: The 3D equivalent of a pixel in volumetric data is called a ___.
exercises.explain: A voxel represents a value on a regular grid in three-dimensional space.

exercises.prompt: Why is 3D segmentation valuable for organ volume measurement?

options.text: It captures the organ's continuous structure across all cross-section slices
It automatically converts the scan to a 2D bounding box
It runs on low-power mobile devices

exercises.explain: By processing the entire volume, it measures organic shapes without slice-by-slice errors.

exercises.prompt: You are training a 3D U-Net on a GPU with 16GB of memory. The model immediately crashes with an 'Out of Memory' error on epoch 1. What should you do?

options.text: Reduce the batch size or crop the large 3D scans into smaller volumetric patches (e.g. 64x64x64) during training.
Switch the model to use 2D max pooling layers.
Increase the learning rate to skip the memory check.
Flatten the 3D volume into a 1D vector and run dense layers.

exercises.explain: Perfect! Volumetric data is extremely memory-intensive. Cropping scans into smaller 3D patches or lowering the batch size is the standard way to fit 3D models onto standard GPUs. Next: let's look at medical imbalances!
